ops.title: Weak-model ccx value: accuracy edge, token-neutral — Design C deferred
ops.body:
# Weak-model ccx value: accuracy edge, token-neutral — Design C deferred

The 2026-07-13 haiku campaign (310 runs, $23.79, session `bench/results/20260713T083836Z`; classic 26 tasks + flood-t5 family, baseline vs ccx-cli, 5 repeats, released ccx 0.13.0 with terse defaults) closed the measurement program's last open prediction.

## The measured result

- **Peak context H: +5.1% [−1.3, +12.4]** — the only non-loss token result in the whole program. Point-positive, CI grazes zero: the peak-context tax that ccx pays on sonnet/opus disappears on haiku.
- **Total tokens T: −17.2% [−41.7, +3.7]** — unresolved. The strong claim ("ccx saves tokens for weak models") is NOT confirmed.
- **Accuracy: ccx-cli 86.6% vs baseline 81.3%** — +5.3 points, the largest edge of any campaign (sonnet: +3.7, opus: +2.3).
- Behavior matched the flood-audit prediction directionally: haiku baselines flood at 30% (sonnet: 17%), and haiku ignores the ccx ladder more (20/310 integrity exclusions vs sonnet's 8/450).

## The deployable claim (state it exactly this way)

On weak models ccx is **token-neutral and buys its largest accuracy improvement**; on strong models it **costs tokens for a smaller accuracy edge**. Do not claim session-level token savings on any measured model — that claim died across sonnet, opus, and two flood-task iterations (see `bench/FINDINGS.md`).

## Design C status

Deferred indefinitely — on evidence, not by measurement of Design C itself (it never ran). The deferral note in FINDINGS names its reopen signal: a weak-model campaign showing real session-level savings. This campaign did NOT produce that signal (T unresolved, H merely parity-to-positive), so the deferral stands. Stage 1's mechanism also cuts against it: compact views withhold members exactly when work demands exhaustive reading, and retained-context compounding could amplify that trade.

## Before optimizing tokens again

Read `bench/FINDINGS.md` first. The instrument (arms isolation, integrity gates, billed-T accounting, paired stats) is sound and reusable; the corpus/task designs are what kept failing to show savings. Any new token claim needs a regime where baselines actually flood — the real-session ceiling for guard rewrites is 3.6–3.9% of billed T, concentrated in a read-heavy tail.
ops.when: before pursuing session-level token optimizations for ccx, revisiting Design C (session-capacity work orders), or characterizing ccx's value on weak models
